title: Genesis 19
subtitle: The Destruction of Sodom; Lot's Escape

map_story_link.text: See on Map — Destruction of Sodom

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — The Angels at Sodom; Lot's Hospitality

poi.name: Sodom — City Gate
poi.text: Lot is at the city gate when the angels arrive — the gate being the civic and judicial centre of ancient cities. His position there suggests full civic integration, not peripheral residence. His hospitality mirrors Abraham's but in an urban rather than pastoral setting.

notes.note: MacArthur: Lot's hospitality to the two angels (v.2-3) mirrors Abraham's (18:1-8) but with differences: Lot is sitting at the gate (a position of civic authority), he bows with his face to the ground, and he urges them strongly after they initially decline. The hospitality is genuine; but Lot's position in Sodom is the source of the coming complication.

MacArthur: The Sodomites' demand to 'know' (yada -- sexual knowledge) the visitors is unambiguous. Lot's offer of his daughters to protect his guests, while following ancient hospitality customs, is morally reprehensible by any standard. The angelic blinding of the crowd (v.11) is the divine protection that overrides the human failure.

heb.paragraph: The two "angels" who arrive at Sodom are called malʾākîm in v.1 but ʾanāšîm ("men") throughout the rest of the chapter — the same ambiguity as in ch.18. The narrative oscillates between their angelic identity and human appearance, creating the theological irony of Sodom's attempted violation: the town literally attempts to assault the messengers of God. The contrast with Abraham's hospitality (18:1-8) is stark — the same divine messengers receive radically different treatment.

The men of Sodom are described as "evil, sinning greatly against the LORD" (13:13), and ch.19 demonstrates the content: violent assault on guests (vv.4-9), contempt for the protective obligations of hospitality, and blindness after being struck (v.11) that is both literal and metaphorical. Ezekiel 16:49-50 identifies Sodom's sin comprehensively: "pride, excess of food and prosperous ease... she did not aid the poor and needy... they did detestable things." Sexual violence is the culmination, not the whole, of Sodom's moral disorder.

The angels "struck the men who were at the door of the house, young and old, with blindness (sannĕwērîm)" (v.11) — a blindness so complete that "they wore themselves out trying to find the door." The word sannĕwērîm (a rare Aramaic loanword, also in 2 Kgs 6:18) describes total incapacitation. The blindness is both the immediate punishment and a symbol of the spiritual blindness that has characterised Sodom throughout. Those who pursue the darkness become blind — they literally cannot find the door.

hist.context: Ezekiel 16:49–50 provides the most detailed biblical analysis of Sodom's sin: "arrogance, overfed and unconcerned; they did not help the poor and needy. They were haughty and did detestable things before me." Homosexual gang rape is the culminating expression of a society organized around pride, comfort, and contempt for the vulnerable. The hospitality violation — attacking guests — is the specific act that triggers judgment in the narrative.

refs.note: Ezekiel's summary of Sodom's sins: pride, excess, neglect of the poor — with the sexual violence as the culmination of a corrupt society.
Jude describes Sodom and Gomorrah as undergoing "the punishment of eternal fire" as an example for those who pursue "sexual immorality and strange flesh."
Jesus uses Sodom as the epitome of judgment, saying it will be "more tolerable for Sodom" than for cities that reject the gospel — making the Sodom account about reception of divine messengers.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The assault of the men of Sodom on the two visitors is the confirmation of the wickedness announced in 13:13 and 18:20. Sarna: "The narrative does not linger on the sin's specifics — the point is the totality of the corruption: 'all the men from every part of the city of Sodom' (v.4). The society has no remnant of righteousness; even the young and old participate. The judgment is therefore complete and just."

Lot's offer of his daughters to the mob is morally scandalous to modern readers. Sarna: "The offer reflects the extreme importance of hospitality obligations in ancient culture — the guest under one's roof must be protected even at great personal cost. The narrative does not endorse Lot's offer; it records it as the desperate measure of a man who has not yet found the exit. The angels resolve the crisis by blinding the mob (v.11), superseding Lot's morally compromised solution."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lot sitting at the gate of Sodom signals his civic integration — he is a judge or elder of the city. Alter: "Lot has completed his journey from outsider (13:12: 'pitched his tents near Sodom') to resident to city official. The man who chose by sight and moved toward Sodom has become, by degrees, one of Sodom's own. The hospitality he extends to the angels is admirable; the city he represents is irredeemable."

The Sodomites turn on Lot: "this fellow came here as a foreigner, and now he wants to play the judge!" Alter: "The men of Sodom expose the contradiction in Lot's position: he has integrated enough to sit at their gate but retains enough moral sense to object to their behavior. He is neither fully theirs nor fully apart from them — the ambiguous position that will mark him through the rest of the chapter."

The sin of Sodom in this passage is the attempted sexual assault of the divine visitors — a sin of violent domination, not merely of sexual immorality. Calvin: "The men of Sodom wished to offer violence to the angels. This is the ultimate degradation of the image of God — to treat divine messengers as objects of violent lust." The sin is simultaneously against creation order, against hospitality, and against the divine presence.

The blinding of the men of Sodom is a mercy as well as a judgment — it gives them an opportunity to stop, which they do not take ("they wore themselves out trying to find the door"). Calvin: "Even when struck blind, they persist in their wickedness. This shows the extent of the corruption: not even divine intervention from outside can redirect a will entirely given over to evil."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "know them" (neda) discusses whether the verb implies sexual knowledge here or simply acquaintance. The note concludes that the context — the contrast with Lot's offer of his daughters — makes the sexual meaning unmistakable. The note also observes that Ezekiel 16:49-50 gives a broader characterisation of Sodom's sins (pride, excess food, unconcern for the poor) that complements rather than replaces the sexual sin described here.

The NET note on Lot's offer of his daughters discusses the legal protection of guests in the ancient Near East: the host was obligated to protect his guests at significant personal cost. The note also discusses the blindness inflicted on the crowd as divine intervention protecting the messengers.

sections.header: Verses 12–22 — Lot's Family Warned; the Escape to Zoar

poi.name: Zoar
poi.coords: Small city south of the Dead Sea
poi.text: Lot names Zoar ("small") while fleeing — the place is saved from destruction at his request. Zoar is the escape valve: the small place spared so the covenant margins are preserved. Lot's retreat to Zoar and then to the mountains traces the withdrawal from civilization.

notes.note: MacArthur: Lot's sons-in-law thought he was joking -- a tragic detail. The man who had chosen Sodom for its prosperity had so compromised his witness that those closest to him could not take his warning seriously. Lot's ineffective warning to his sons-in-law illustrates how worldly compromise destroys prophetic credibility.

MacArthur: 'I cannot do anything until you reach it' -- the divine limitation on judgment is imposed by the covenant obligation to rescue the righteous before executing judgment on the wicked. The timing of Sodom's destruction is governed by Lot's safety, not by any independent divine timetable.

heb.paragraph: The angels' repeated command to "hurry" (māhar, vv.15, 17, 22) and Lot's delay (v.16 — "he hesitated") creates the chapter's central irony: the rescuers must drag the rescued toward their own salvation. "The LORD was merciful to them" (v.16) — the causative of the verb leads them by the hand even when they are too spiritually attached to Sodom to flee voluntarily. The same dynamic characterises God's patient leading of Israel toward what they would not independently choose.

"Flee for your lives! Don't look back, and don't stop" (v.17) — the command has the same urgency as the Exodus departure instructions: "Leave Egypt in haste" (Exod 12:11). The specification "don't look back" (ʾal-tabiṭ ʾaḥărêkā) is the prohibition of nostalgia for what is being left behind. Lot's wife looks back (v.26 — watabbēṭ ʾiššāh ʾaḥărāyw) and becomes a "pillar of salt" — petrified in the act of return. Jesus cites her as a warning against attachment to what God is calling us to leave (Luke 17:32).

The chapter ends with the birth of Moab and Ben-Ammi from Lot's daughters (vv.37-38) — the ancestors of the Moabites and Ammonites, nations that will have complex relationships with Israel throughout its history. Ruth is a Moabite; Naamah the Ammonite is a wife of Solomon. The genealogical note is not merely historical but theological: even these nations, born of desperate and compromised circumstances, fall within the scope of God's historical purposes.

hist.context: Lot's rescue is explicitly credited to Abraham's intercession in v.29 — "God remembered Abraham and sent Lot out." The intercession of ch.18 had its effect not in sparing Sodom but in sparing Lot. The divine mercy operates through the covenant relationship even when the direct prayer is not answered as asked. Lot's wife's backward look is interpreted by Jesus as a warning: "Remember Lot's wife" (Luke 17:32) — clinging to what God has condemned means sharing its fate.

refs.note: Jesus uses Sodom's destruction as a type of eschatological judgment, warning "Remember Lot's wife" — do not look back when the Son of Man comes.

Peter: God "condemned the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah...making them an example of what is going to happen to the ungodly; and if he rescued Lot, a righteous man...then the Lord knows how to rescue the godly from trials."

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lot's sons-in-law think he is joking — "he seemed to his sons-in-law to be jesting." Sarna: "The irony is devastating: the man who has the divine warning, who knows the truth, is not believed by those closest to him. His credibility in Sodom has been entirely consumed by his assimilation. He can no longer speak with prophetic authority to those who know him."

Lot's negotiation with the angel — asking to flee to Zoar rather than to the mountains — shows the persistence of his character: even in a life-and-death flight from divine judgment, he bargains. Sarna: "Lot's request for Zoar ('little one') is granted. The irony of Lot's career is contained in this small negotiation: even at the moment of rescue, he seeks the smallest possible accommodation rather than full separation from the cities of the plain."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lot lingers — the angel seizes him, his wife, and his daughters by the hand. Alter: "The physical seizure of Lot's family is one of the most tender moments in the judgment narrative: divine grace literally dragging the saved out of the path of destruction. Lot, who has been unable to flee on his own, is bodily carried out by angelic hands. The salvation is entirely from outside."

Lot's negotiation for Zoar — 'let me flee there' — is characteristic of his half-obedience throughout the narrative. He was told to flee to the mountains; he bargains for a city instead. Alter notes Lot trusts the divine rescue but not the divine instruction about where to go.

The angel's seizure of Lot's family despite Lot's hesitation is the acting out of divine mercy toward the undeserving. Calvin: "Lot, notwithstanding the revelation of God and the proximity of the judgment, was so seized with torpor that he had to be forcibly dragged away. This shows us how much the salvation of the godly depends on the grace of God alone, and not on human promptness or merit."

Calvin: Lot had received a clear command but substituted his own judgment. He was afraid of the mountains and asked for a city instead. God grants the request out of mercy — not because Lot's judgment was right but because divine condescension extends even to those who comply imperfectly.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "flee for your lives! Don't look back and don't stop anywhere in the plain!" discusses the narrative function of these commands: they set up both Lot's wife's looking back (v.26) and Lot's own hesitation to reach the mountains (v.19). The imperative structure makes the subsequent failures more culpable — both Lot's wife and Lot himself are acting against explicit divine command.

The NET note on 'don't look back' discusses the command as requiring full commitment to the rescue. Lot's wife's transformation into a salt pillar is the physical consequence of the spiritual condition her gaze expressed: she could not leave Sodom behind.

sections.header: Verses 23–38 — Sodom Destroyed; Lot's Wife; Lot and His Daughters

poi.name: Sodom and Gomorrah
poi.text: The destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah is one of the most geologically dramatic events in the OT — fire and brimstone from the sky, the smoke rising like a furnace. The Dead Sea region's sulphur and bitumen deposits make this the most plausible geographic candidate for the cities.

poi.name: Lot's Cave / Mountains of Moab
poi.text: Lot and his daughters retreat to the mountains east of Zoar — the territory that will become Moab and Ammon. The cave-birth of Moab and Ammon in the aftermath of Sodom's destruction ties these nations' origins to judgment's margins.

notes.note: MacArthur: Lot's wife looked back and became a pillar of salt. Jesus cites this in Luke 17:32 ('Remember Lot's wife') as a warning about attachment to the world when the call to escape is given. The backward look was the physical expression of a heart that had not fully left Sodom.

MacArthur: The Moabites and Ammonites, Israel's persistent neighbours and adversaries, descend from Lot's incestuous unions with his daughters. The origin story explains both the kinship (they are Abraham's relatives) and the tension (their origin in moral compromise shapes their character as peoples). Ruth the Moabitess will later demonstrate that this ancestry is not destiny.

heb.paragraph: "The LORD rained down burning sulphur (gāprît) on Sodom and Gomorrah" (v.24) — gāprît is volcanic/geological material associated with divine judgment throughout Scripture (Deut 29:23; Ps 11:6; Isa 34:9; Rev 19:20; 21:8). The destruction is total — "dense smoke, like smoke from a furnace" (v.28). Archaeological and geological evidence around the Dead Sea region suggests a catastrophic geological event in the Middle Bronze Age. The narrative presents the event as direct divine action — mediated through natural processes but theologically purposive.

Lot's wife "became a pillar of salt" (v.26) — a detail so specific that it preserved in local memory the landscape feature it supposedly explained. But the theological function is more important than the geological: she looked back and was destroyed by what she looked back at. Salt in the OT has dual significance: it is the covenant mineral (Num 18:19 — "a covenant of salt forever") and the symbol of desolation (Deut 29:23; Judg 9:45). She is petrified in covenant violation — the permanent monument to the failure of her faith.

"God remembered Abraham, and he brought Lot out of the catastrophe" (v.29) — the same divine zākar that opened the Flood's turning point (8:1: "God remembered Noah"). Divine remembering in the OT is active, purposive covenantal faithfulness. Lot is saved not because of his own merit — the narrative has repeatedly shown his moral ambiguity — but "because God remembered Abraham." The salvation of the marginal figure through the merit of the covenant patriarch is a theological pattern that runs from Lot through Ruth through every sinner sheltered under Christ's covenant.

hist.context: Lot's rescue is explicitly credited to Abraham's intercession in v.29 — "God remembered Abraham and sent Lot out." The intercession of ch.18 had its effect not in sparing Sodom but in sparing Lot. The divine mercy operates through the covenant relationship even when the direct prayer is not answered as asked. Lot's wife's backward look is interpreted by Jesus as a warning: "Remember Lot's wife" (Luke 17:32) — clinging to what God has condemned means sharing its fate.

refs.note: Jesus uses Sodom's destruction as a type of eschatological judgment, warning "Remember Lot's wife" — do not look back when the Son of Man comes.

Peter: God "condemned the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah...making them an example of what is going to happen to the ungodly; and if he rescued Lot, a righteous man...then the Lord knows how to rescue the godly from trials."

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah by "burning sulfur" — brimstone and fire — is the most dramatic divine judgment in the pre-Mosaic narrative. Sarna: "Archaeological surveys of the Dead Sea region have identified evidence of violent geological activity and conflagration that may be consistent with the narrative. Whatever the physical mechanism, the text presents the destruction as a direct divine act — 'the LORD rained down burning sulfur from the LORD out of the heavens.'"

Lot's wife looking back and becoming a pillar of salt is an etiological element — explaining a feature of the Dead Sea landscape. Sarna: "The story is not merely punitive; it illustrates the fatal attachment to what is being left behind. She looked back not merely to see but to remain — her whole orientation was still toward Sodom, and she became what she was already turning back toward."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: "But Lot's wife looked back, and she became a pillar of salt." Alter: "One of the most economical sentences in Genesis. No motive is given, no explanation, no moral judgment — only the action and its consequence. The reader is left to interpret: curiosity? longing? disobedience? The refusal to explain is part of the horror. She looked back; she is salt. The terseness is the judgment."

The Lot-daughters episode is deeply uncomfortable. Alter: "The daughters' plan — to preserve their father's line by making him drunk and conceiving — is presented without direct moral evaluation. Their motivation ('there is no man anywhere to sleep with us') is based on the false assumption that all men have perished. The children born — Moab and Ben-Ammi — are the ancestors of Israel's persistent adversaries. The episode is the dark comedy at the end of Lot's story."

Lot's wife as a warning. Calvin: "She perished in punishment of her unbelief. Therefore, when the Lord calls us to leave the world and to be separated from it, let us not look back; for, though the Lord is willing to pardon many faults in his people, yet he is severe in punishing the treachery of those who, after they have been delivered, look back toward the destruction from which they have been rescued."

Calvin on the incest narrative: "This dreadful, horrible history is reported by Moses, not to excuse the crime of Lot's daughters, but to show how sadly the human mind is corrupted when it turns away from God's word, and how great evils follow from dwelling among the wicked. Lot had yielded too much to the influence of Sodom, and now he suffers the consequence in his own family."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "burning sulfur from the LORD out of the heavens" discusses the natural disaster interpretation (volcanic/geological activity in the Dead Sea rift) vs. direct divine action. The note observes that the text attributes the event directly to YHWH regardless of the physical mechanism. The note also notes the unusual phrase "the LORD rained down from the LORD" — which some read as evidence of the distinction between YHWH and the Angel of YHWH.

The NET note on Lot's wife becoming "a pillar of salt" discusses whether this is literal or figurative. The note notes the geological features of the Dead Sea region (salt formations, salt pillars) and observes that the text presents the event as a divine act of judgment. The note connects to Luke 17:32 — "Remember Lot's wife" — where Jesus uses her as a warning about looking back when judgment comes.

ppl.name: Lot
ppl.text: Called "righteous" by 2 Peter 2:7–8, yet he lingered (v.16), offered his daughters to the mob (v.8), and needed to be seized by angels to escape. His righteousness is real but barely adequate — he is saved "as through fire" (1 Cor 3:15).

ppl.name: Lot's Wife
ppl.role: Unnamed; became a pillar of salt
ppl.text: Her backward glance (v.26) has generated enormous interpretive attention. Jesus warns: "Remember Lot's wife" (Luke 17:32). The salt pillar may be historical (the Lisan Peninsula area has salt formations) or symbolic of petrification in the moment of disobedience.

ppl.name: Two Angels
ppl.text: The same figures as two of the three Mamre visitors. They execute divine judgment with decisive efficiency — and merciful persistence in rescuing Lot despite his hesitation.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"So when God destroyed the cities of the plain, he remembered Abraham, and he brought Lot out of the catastrophe that overthrew the cities where Lot had lived."ESV"So it was that, when God destroyed the cities of the valley, God remembered Abraham and sent Lot out of the midst of the overthrow when he overthrew the cities in which Lot had lived."KJV"And it came to pass, when God destroyed the cities of the plain, that God remembered Abraham, and sent Lot out of the midst of the overthrow, when he overthrew the cities in which Lot dwelt."

src.title: Tall el-Hammam Archaeological Evidence (2021)
src.quote: A 2021 Nature Scientific Reports article by Bunch et al. described evidence of a catastrophic cosmic airburst (similar to the Tunguska event) at Tall el-Hammam c. 1650 BC — melted pottery, shocked quartz, platinum spike consistent with an extraterrestrial impact. The city showed sudden total destruction.
src.note: The findings do not prove the biblical account but provide a possible natural mechanism for a catastrophic destruction event in exactly the right geographical and chronological range.

rec.text: Sodom becomes the stock image of divine judgment: Isaiah 1:9–10 calls Jerusalem's leaders "rulers of Sodom"; Jeremiah 23:14 says Jerusalem's prophets are "like Sodom"; Amos 4:11 uses Sodom as a benchmark of God's past warnings. The city's destruction permeates prophetic imagination.
The great city where the two witnesses are killed is called "spiritually...Sodom and Egypt, where their Lord was crucified" — apocalyptic Sodom becomes a symbol of any anti-God civilization.

rows.label: Hospitality vs Violation at the Door
rows.text: Lot's hospitality and the city's violation demand confront each other at the door. The divine blinding of the crowd is the direct divine intervention that protects the messengers -- and reveals the city's judgment.

rows.label: The Backward Look: Salt as Monument to Attachment
rows.text: She looked back. The brevity of the sentence is the economy of judgment. She could not leave Sodom behind; the city she could not release became her monument. Attachment that cannot release the past becomes the past.

hebtext.note: The two "angels" who arrive at Sodom are called malʾākîm in v.1 but ʾanāšîm ("men") throughout the rest of the chapter — the same ambiguity as in ch.18. The narrative oscillates between their angelic identity and human appearance, creating the theological irony of Sodom's attempted violation: the town literally attempts to assault the messengers of God. The contrast with Abraham's hospitality (18:1-8) is stark — the same divine messengers receive radically different treatment.

The men of Sodom are described as "evil, sinning greatly against the LORD" (13:13), and ch.19 demonstrates the content: violent assault on guests (vv.4-9), contempt for the protective obligations of hospitality, and blindness after being struck (v.11) that is both literal and metaphorical. Ezekiel 16:49-50 identifies Sodom's sin comprehensively: "pride, excess of food and prosperous ease... she did not aid the poor and needy... they did detestable things." Sexual violence is the culmination, not the whole, of Sodom's moral disorder.

The angels "struck the men who were at the door of the house, young and old, with blindness (sannĕwērîm)" (v.11) — a blindness so complete that "they wore themselves out trying to find the door." The word sannĕwērîm (a rare Aramaic loanword, also in 2 Kgs 6:18) describes total incapacitation. The blindness is both the immediate punishment and a symbol of the spiritual blindness that has characterised Sodom throughout. Those who pursue the darkness become blind — they literally cannot find the door.

The angels' repeated command to "hurry" (māhar, vv.15, 17, 22) and Lot's delay (v.16 — "he hesitated") creates the chapter's central irony: the rescuers must drag the rescued toward their own salvation. "The LORD was merciful to them" (v.16) — the causative of the verb leads them by the hand even when they are too spiritually attached to Sodom to flee voluntarily. The same dynamic characterises God's patient leading of Israel toward what they would not independently choose.

"Flee for your lives! Don't look back, and don't stop" (v.17) — the command has the same urgency as the Exodus departure instructions: "Leave Egypt in haste" (Exod 12:11). The specification "don't look back" (ʾal-tabiṭ ʾaḥărêkā) is the prohibition of nostalgia for what is being left behind. Lot's wife looks back (v.26 — watabbēṭ ʾiššāh ʾaḥărāyw) and becomes a "pillar of salt" — petrified in the act of return. Jesus cites her as a warning against attachment to what God is calling us to leave (Luke 17:32).

The chapter ends with the birth of Moab and Ben-Ammi from Lot's daughters (vv.37-38) — the ancestors of the Moabites and Ammonites, nations that will have complex relationships with Israel throughout its history. Ruth is a Moabite; Naamah the Ammonite is a wife of Solomon. The genealogical note is not merely historical but theological: even these nations, born of desperate and compromised circumstances, fall within the scope of God's historical purposes.

"The LORD rained down burning sulphur (gāprît) on Sodom and Gomorrah" (v.24) — gāprît is volcanic/geological material associated with divine judgment throughout Scripture (Deut 29:23; Ps 11:6; Isa 34:9; Rev 19:20; 21:8). Archaeological and geological evidence around the Dead Sea region suggests a catastrophic geological event in the Middle Bronze Age. The narrative presents the event as direct divine action — mediated through natural processes but theologically purposive.

Lot's wife "became a pillar of salt" (v.26) — a detail so specific that it preserved in local memory the landscape feature it supposedly explained. But the theological function is more important than the geological: she looked back and was destroyed by what she looked back at. Salt in the OT has dual significance: it is the covenant mineral (Num 18:19 — "a covenant of salt forever") and the symbol of desolation (Deut 29:23; Judg 9:45). She is petrified in covenant violation — the permanent monument to the failure of her faith.

"God remembered Abraham, and he brought Lot out of the catastrophe" (v.29) — the same divine zākar that opened the Flood's turning point (8:1: "God remembered Noah"). Divine remembering in the OT is active, purposive covenantal faithfulness. Lot is saved not because of his own merit — the narrative has repeatedly shown his moral ambiguity — but "because God remembered Abraham." The salvation of the marginal figure through the merit of the covenant patriarch is a theological pattern that runs from Lot through Ruth through every sinner sheltered under Christ's covenant.

tx.title: Lot's offer — textual unanimity
tx.content: Lot's deeply troubling offer of his daughters is textually stable across MT, SP, LXX, and Targum Onqelos. No ancient witness softens, omits, or modifies the passage. Targum Onqelos translates straightforwardly without the paraphrastic deflection it applies to other morally difficult passages.
tx.note: The textual unanimity is itself significant — ancient scribes preserved the passage unaltered despite its moral difficulty, a testament to the conservative nature of textual transmission.

tx.title: God 'remembered' Abraham — LXX expansion
tx.content: MT states simply that God remembered Abraham and sent Lot out. The LXX adds a clause specifying that God remembered Abraham's intercession (from ch. 18), making the connection between the two narratives explicit. The MT leaves the connection implicit.
tx.note: The LXX addition is a typical clarifying expansion — making explicit what the MT narrator trusts the reader to infer. This pattern recurs throughout LXX Genesis.

debate.title: The Sin of Sodom: Sexual or Social?

positions.name: Sexual/homosexual
positions.argument: The mob demands to "know" (yada) the men sexually (v.5). The same verb is used in v.8 for the daughters "who have not known a man." The sexual connotation is explicit. The sin includes attempted homosexual gang rape.

positions.name: Inhospitality / violence
positions.argument: The verb yada (know) can mean "become acquainted with" (Gen 29:5). The sin is violation of hospitality norms, not sexual. Ezekiel 16:49 identifies Sodom's sin as pride, gluttony, and neglect of the poor — with no sexual reference.

positions.name: Both/comprehensive
positions.argument: Jude 7 ("gave themselves up to sexual immorality and perversion") confirms a sexual element. But the broader prophetic condemnation (Ezek 16:49; Isa 1:10-17; Amos 4:11) emphasises social sins. Sodom's wickedness was comprehensive.
